sections.content: In this exercise, you will build a complete PyTorch neural network system that predicts cricket player performance metrics (runs scored, strike rate, and match outcome probability) based on historical innings data. You'll implement a multi-layer feedforward network with dropout regularization, custom loss functions that handle classification and regression simultaneously, and a training pipeline with validation monitoring. The system will process real-world cricket statistics (balls faced, boundaries hit, dots played, powerplay runs) and output both continuous predictions (expected runs) and probabilistic classifications (win likelihood). This requires building dataset loaders, constructing the neural network architecture with appropriate activation functions, implementing batch normalization, and creating an evaluation framework that measures both regression error (MAE) and classification accuracy. The project demonstrates how deep learning handles mixed prediction tasks in sports analytics, where you must simultaneously predict numeric performance and categorical outcomes from structured tabular data.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Virat Kohli walking to the crease with a specific data pattern — his recent form, the bowler's recent economy rate, the pitch conditions, and the match situation. Just as Kohli's brain instantly processes these signals and decides his batting approach (aggressive in powerplay, cautious against yorkers, rotating strike), your neural network learns to recognize patterns in player statistics and make predictions about outcomes. The input layer is like scouts gathering information about pitch behavior and player form; the hidden layers are like Kohli's mental processing, where different aspects of the situation are weighted and combined; the output layer is like his final decision — will he score runs (continuous prediction) and can the team win from here (classification)? Dropout is like how Kohli occasionally ignores one piece of information to avoid over-relying on recent trends (overfitting to last game's conditions). Batch normalization is like normalizing the pressure environment — whether you're playing at the MCG or a small ground, you standardize your mental approach so the core decision-making stays consistent. Understanding this flow reveals why neural networks need both wide information gathering (many input features), careful processing (hidden layers with proper sizing), and decisive output (clear prediction targets).

items: Solid understanding of PyTorch tensors, autograd, and basic neural network construction with nn.Module
Knowledge of forward propagation, backpropagation, and optimization algorithms (SGD, Adam, momentum mechanics)
Familiarity with loss functions, metric evaluation (MAE, MSE, accuracy), and training/validation splits in deep learning
Experience with Python data manipulation using NumPy and Pandas for dataset preprocessing and feature normalization
Understanding of regularization techniques (L1/L2, dropout) and batch normalization to prevent overfitting and stabilize training

sections.content: You will organize the cricket prediction project into a clean, modular structure with separate directories for data, models, utilities, and results. Create a virtual environment to isolate dependencies, then install PyTorch (CPU or GPU), NumPy, Pandas, Scikit-learn, and Matplotlib. The project directory will contain data preprocessing scripts, the neural network model definition, training and evaluation scripts, and a results folder for saving checkpoints and prediction outputs. This structure mimics production deep learning workflows where code is organized for reproducibility, scalability, and team collaboration. You'll generate synthetic cricket datasets with realistic player statistics, ensuring proper train-validation-test splits and feature normalization before feeding data into the network.

# Create project directory structure for cricket performance prediction
mkdir -p cricket_performance_predictor/{data,models,utils,results,scripts}
cd cricket_performance_predictor

# Create virtual environment
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

# Install required dependencies
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
pip install numpy pandas scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn jupyter

# Create initial Python files
touch data/generate_cricket_data.py
touch models/cricket_nn.py
touch utils/preprocessing.py
touch utils/metrics.py
touch scripts/train.py
touch scripts/evaluate.py
touch README.md

# Verify structure
tree . # or ls -la for directory listing

echo "Cricket Performance Predictor project structure created successfully!"

The foundation step establishes the data pipeline by generating realistic cricket player statistics and implementing proper preprocessing techniques. You'll create a synthetic dataset containing 500+ player innings with features like balls faced, boundaries hit, dots played, powerplay runs, total runs, and match outcome. The preprocessing step normalizes all numeric features using StandardScaler (zero mean, unit variance) to ensure the neural network receives inputs on comparable scales — this is critical because raw feature values (e.g., balls: 120, runs: 87) have different ranges and can cause training instability. You'll implement train-validation-test splits (70-20-10 ratio) with stratification by match outcome to maintain class distribution. Finally, you'll convert NumPy arrays into PyTorch tensors with appropriate data types (float32 for features, long for classification targets) and create custom Dataset and DataLoader classes that handle batching and shuffling. This foundation determines model performance quality because poor preprocessing compounds through every training iteration.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Before an international cricket match, the team goes through a rigorous preparation phase — groundstaff prepare the pitch, the team reviews opposition strengths, physios condition players, and analysts study recent form. This data generation and preprocessing is exactly that preparation phase. Creating the dataset is like gathering scouting reports on 500+ recent innings from various batters; each innings record (balls, runs, boundaries) is like a detailed match report. Normalization (StandardScaler) is like putting all players on equal footing — whether a player is physically larger or has unorthodox technique, the coach standardizes training methods so position and fitness variations don't skew analysis. The train-validation-test split is like dividing your preparation into phases: first 70% of prep matches to learn (training), middle 20% as test matches to validate readiness (validation), final 10% as the actual international series (testing — unseen). Stratification by outcome is like ensuring your practice matches include wins AND losses, so you're prepared for both scenarios, not just practices against weak opponents. DataLoaders with shuffling are like randomizing opponent order during practice so players don't develop predictable patterns. Understanding data preprocessing reveals that model quality is fundamentally limited by input quality — garbage in, garbage out, so preparation is where championships are actually won.

# data/generate_cricket_data.py
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
import os

class CricketDataset(Dataset):
    """Custom PyTorch Dataset for cricket performance data"""
    def __init__(self, features, regression_target, classification_target):
        self.features = torch.FloatTensor(features)
        self.runs = torch.FloatTensor(regression_target).unsqueeze(1)  # Shape: (N, 1)
        self.win = torch.LongTensor(classification_target)  # Shape: (N,)
    
    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.features)
    
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.features[idx], self.runs[idx], self.win[idx]

def generate_cricket_data(num_samples=500, random_seed=42):
    """Generate synthetic cricket player performance data"""
    np.random.seed(random_seed)
    
    player_names = [
        'Rohit Sharma', 'Virat Kohli', 'KL Rahul', 'Suryakumar Yadav',
        'Ishan Kishan', 'Hardik Pandya', 'Yuzvendra Chahal', 'Jasprit Bumrah'
    ]
    
    data = []
    
    for _ in range(num_samples):
        balls_faced = np.random.randint(20, 120)
        powerplay_runs = np.random.randint(0, min(balls_faced, 36))
        boundaries_4 = np.random.randint(0, balls_faced // 10)
        boundaries_6 = np.random.randint(0, balls_faced // 15)
        dots_played = np.random.randint(5, min(balls_faced - boundaries_4 - boundaries_6, 80))
        
        # Calculate derived features
        total_runs = powerplay_runs + (boundaries_4 * 4) + (boundaries_6 * 6) + \n                     np.random.randint(max(0, balls_faced - powerplay_runs - 
                                          boundaries_4 - boundaries_6 - dots_played), 
                                       balls_faced - powerplay_runs - boundaries_4 - 
                                       boundary_6 - dots_played + 10)
        strike_rate = (total_runs / balls_faced * 100) if balls_faced > 0 else 0
        
        # Win probability based on runs (simplified: higher runs -> higher win chance)
        win_probability = min(1.0, total_runs / 100 + np.random.normal(0, 0.1))
        match_won = 1 if win_probability > 0.5 else 0
        
        data.append({
            'player': np.random.choice(player_names)
sections.match_won:
match_won
        })
    
    return pd.DataFrame(data)

def preprocess_data(df, test_size=0.1, val_size=0.2):
    """
    Preprocess cricket data: normalize features, split train/val/test,
    return DataLoaders for model training
    """
    # Feature columns and targets
    feature_cols = ['balls_faced', 'powerplay_runs', 'boundaries_4', 
                   'boundaries_6', 'dots_played', 'strike_rate']
    regression_target = 'total_runs'
    classification_target = 'match_won'
    
    X = df[feature_cols].values
    y_runs = df[regression_target].values
    y_win = df[classification_target].values
    
    # Normalize features using StandardScaler
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
    
    # First split: train (70%) vs temp (30%)
    X_train, X_temp, y_runs_train, y_runs_temp, y_win_train, y_win_temp = \n        train_test_split(X_scaled, y_runs, y_win, test_size=test_size + val_size,
                        random_state=42, stratify=y_win)
    
    # Second split: temp into val (20%) and test (10%)
    val_ratio = val_size / (test_size + val_size)
    X_val, X_test, y_runs_val, y_runs_test, y_win_val, y_win_test = \n        train_test_split(X_temp, y_runs_temp, y_win_temp, test_size=1-val_ratio,
                        random_state=42, stratify=y_win_temp)
    
    # Create PyTorch Datasets
    train_dataset = CricketDataset(X_train, y_runs_train, y_win_train)
    val_dataset = CricketDataset(X_val, y_runs_val, y_win_val)
    test_dataset = CricketDataset(X_test, y_runs_test, y_win_test)
    
    # Create DataLoaders with shuffling for training
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
    val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
    test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
    
    return train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, scaler

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Generate and save data
    df = generate_cricket_data(num_samples=500)
    df.to_csv('data/cricket_innings.csv', index=False)
    print(f"Generated {len(df)} cricket innings records")
    print(f"\nDataset head:\n{df.head()}")
    
    # Preprocess and create loaders
    train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, scaler = preprocess_data(df)
    print(f"\nTrain batches: {len(train_loader)}, Val batches: {len(val_loader)}, Test batches: {len(test_loader)}")
    
    # Verify data shapes
    for features, runs, wins in train_loader:
        print(f"\nBatch shapes - Features: {features.shape}

Runs.content: Step 2 — Core Logic: Neural Network Architecture

sections.content: The core logic implements a sophisticated neural network architecture that handles multi-task learning — simultaneously predicting continuous runs (regression) and match outcome (classification). The network architecture consists of a shared input layer (6 features), followed by two hidden layers (128 and 64 neurons) with ReLU activation to introduce non-linearity, batch normalization after each hidden layer to stabilize training and reduce internal covariate shift, and dropout regularization (0.3 probability) to prevent overfitting. The network then splits into two task-specific heads: a regression head that outputs a single continuous value (predicted runs) and a classification head that outputs 2 logits for binary classification (win/loss probability). You'll implement custom forward logic that processes input through shared layers, then branches to separate outputs, enabling the network to learn shared representations while specializing for each prediction task. The loss function combines regression loss (MSE for continuous prediction) with classification loss (CrossEntropyLoss for binary outcome), weighted appropriately to balance both objectives during backpropagation.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a cricket analyst watching a batter — they observe the same raw data (bowler type, pitch conditions, field placement, ball trajectory) and must make two simultaneous predictions: how many runs will this batter score (regression), and will the team win the match (classification). The shared hidden layers are like the analyst's core observation and judgment system — they process all the raw sensory information (neural activation patterns) to identify key patterns. Batch normalization is like standardizing the analyst's evaluation framework — whether watching under stadium lights or overcast conditions, the core judgment criteria remain consistent so conclusions stay reliable. Dropout is like the analyst intentionally ignoring one random piece of information during each decision cycle (during training) to avoid becoming dependent on quirks of specific broadcasts — this makes them more robust to unseen scenarios. The regression head (predicting runs) is like the analyst's run prediction branch — they look at recent ball positions, bat swing patterns, and fielder placement to estimate total runs. The classification head (predicting win) is like their match-outcome prediction branch — it uses the same processed information but answers a different question: given these patterns, does the team win? The weighted loss function is like telling the analyst 'both predictions matter equally, so balance your focus.' Understanding multi-task learning reveals why shared representations (the core processing layers) are powerful — information learned for one task (runs) helps the other task (win prediction) because both depend on similar underlying patterns in player performance.

# models/cricket_nn.py
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class CricketPerformancePredictor(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-task neural network for cricket performance prediction.
    Tasks: 
    1. Regression - predict total runs scored
    2. Classification - predict match win/loss
    """
    
    def __init__(self, input_size=6, hidden1_size=128, hidden2_size=64, 
                 dropout_rate=0.3):
        super(CricketPerformancePredictor, self).__init__()
        
        # Shared layers for feature learning
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden1_size)
        self.bn1 = nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden1_size)
        self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(dropout_rate)
        
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden1_size, hidden2_size)
        self.bn2 = nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden2_size)
        self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(dropout_rate)
        
        # Regression head: predict total runs
        self.fc_regression = nn.Linear(hidden2_size, 32)
        self.fc_regression_out = nn.Linear(32, 1)
        
        # Classification head: predict win/loss (2 classes)
        self.fc_classification = nn.Linear(hidden2_size, 32)
        self.fc_classification_out = nn.Linear(32, 2)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        """
        Forward pass through the network.
        
        Args:
            x: Input tensor of shape (batch_size, input_size)
            
        Returns:
            Tuple of (regression_output, classification_output)
            - regression_output: (batch_size, 1) - predicted runs
            - classification_output: (batch_size, 2) - logits for win/loss
        """
        # Shared feature learning path
        shared = F.relu(self.bn1(self.fc1(x)))
        shared = self.dropout1(shared)
        
        shared = F.relu(self.bn2(self.fc2(shared)))
        shared = self.dropout2(shared)
        
        # Regression branch: predict total runs
        regression_hidden = F.relu(self.fc_regression(shared))
        runs_predicted = self.fc_regression_out(regression_hidden)  # No activation, continuous output
        
        # Classification branch: predict win/loss
        classification_hidden = F.relu(self.fc_classification(shared))
        win_logits = self.fc_classification_out(classification_hidden)  # Raw logits for CrossEntropyLoss
        
        return runs_predicted, win_logits

class CricketLossFunction(nn.Module):
    """
    Combined loss function for multi-task learning.
    Balances regression (MSE for runs) and classification (CrossEntropy for win)
    """
    
    def __init__(self, regression_weight=1.0, classification_weight=1.0):
        super(CricketLossFunction, self).__init__()
        self.regression_weight = regression_weight
        self.classification_weight = classification_weight
        self.mse_loss = nn.MSELoss()
        self.ce_loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
    
    def forward(self, runs_pred, runs_true, win_logits, win_true):
        """
        Calculate combined loss.
        
        Args:
            runs_pred: Predicted runs, shape (batch_size, 1)
            runs_true: True runs, shape (batch_size, 1)
            win_logits: Classification logits, shape (batch_size, 2)
            win_true: True win labels, shape (batch_size,)
            
        Returns:
            Total loss (scalar)
        """
        # Regression loss: MSE between predicted and actual runs
        runs_true = runs_true.squeeze()  # Ensure shape (batch_size,)
        regression_loss = self.mse_loss(runs_pred.squeeze(), runs_true)
        
        # Classification loss: CrossEntropy for win/loss prediction
        classification_loss = self.ce_loss(win_logits, win_true)
        
        # Weighted sum of both losses
        total_loss = (self.regression_weight * regression_loss + 
                     self.classification_weight * classification_loss)
        
        return total_loss, regression_loss, classification_loss

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Test model instantiation and forward pass
    model = CricketPerformancePredictor(input_size=6, hidden1_size=128, 
                                       hidden2_size=64, dropout_rate=0.3)
    
    # Create dummy input (batch_size=4, input_size=6)
    dummy_input = torch.randn(4, 6)
    
    # Forward pass
    runs_out, win_out = model(dummy_input)
    print(f"Model Architecture:\n{model}")
    print(f"\nDummy input shape: {dummy_input.shape}")
    print(f"Runs prediction shape: {runs_out.shape}")
    print(f"Win classification logits shape: {win_out.shape}")
    
    # Test loss function
    loss_fn = CricketLossFunction(regression_weight=1.0, classification_weight=1.0)
    dummy_runs_true = torch.randn(4, 1)
    dummy_win_true = torch.LongTensor([0, 1, 1, 0])
    
    total_loss, reg_loss, clf_loss = loss_fn(runs_out, dummy_runs_true, 
                                             win_out, dummy_win_true)
    print(f"\nTotal Loss: {total_loss.item():.4f}")
    print(f"Regression Loss: {reg_loss.item():.4f}")
    print(f"Classification Loss: {clf_loss.item():.4f}")

Integration and enhancement brings together data preprocessing, model architecture, and loss functions into a complete training pipeline. You'll implement a comprehensive training loop that iterates through epochs, processes batches from the DataLoader, computes forward passes through the network, calculates multi-task losses, performs backpropagation to update weights, and tracks metrics across training and validation datasets. The pipeline includes checkpoint saving to persist the best model (based on validation loss), learning rate scheduling to adjust optimization dynamics during training, early stopping to prevent overfitting, and detailed logging of regression metrics (MAE, RMSE) and classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall). You'll also implement an evaluation function that runs inference on the test set without gradient computation, generating predictions for both runs and match outcomes. The enhancement step introduces techniques like gradient clipping to prevent exploding gradients, validation-based model selection to ensure generalization, and visualization of training curves to diagnose learning dynamics. This integrated pipeline transforms isolated components into a production-ready system that can train robust cricket performance predictors.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Building a training pipeline is like preparing a cricket team for an international series. Data loading is like the team organization — you divide players into batting and bowling units, assign roles, and schedule practice sessions (batches). The training epoch is like a full-day practice match: batters face real deliveries (forward pass), the team reviews performance (loss calculation), coaches provide feedback (backpropagation), and players adjust technique (weight updates). Validation during training is like mid-series assessments — after each practice match, the captain evaluates which players are match-ready and which need more work, without playing them in actual international matches yet. Learning rate scheduling is like adjusting training intensity — early in preparation (high learning rate), you want intense fitness drills and tactical experiments; as the series approaches (lower learning rate), you focus on fine-tuning and recovery. Early stopping is like the coach saying 'we're getting fatigued and dropping performance; let's stop practice and rest' — if validation performance plateaus or degrades, continued training becomes counterproductive. Checkpoint saving is like archiving the team's peak fitness state — you save the model when validation is best, so if players regress later, you can return to that optimal state. Test set evaluation is like the actual international match — you only evaluate on unseen opponents once, in final tournament conditions. Understanding integrated training reveals why individual components matter less than orchestration; a great architecture with poor training becomes mediocre, while adequate architecture with excellent training becomes formidable.
